nativeRange.habitat: Coastal rivers and smaller tributaries of northeastern Brazil, including the rios Parnaíba, Guamá, Maracanã, and Mearim; typically inhabits shallow, slow-moving water over fine sandy or muddy substrate

diet.notes: Readily accepts sinking pellets, wafers, frozen bloodworms, tubifex, brine shrimp, and daphnia. Has been documented rooting through substrate for food using sensitive barbels. Should not be expected to subsist on leftover food from surface feeders — dedicated bottom feeding is required.

summary: The Julii Cory is a small, peaceful schooling catfish from northeastern Brazil, known for its fine, isolated dark spots. Most fish sold under this name are actually Corydoras trilineatus; true Corydoras julii is considerably rarer in the trade. Groups of six or more are recommended. Sensitive to copper-based medications.
careNotes:
WIDESPREAD TRADE CONFUSION: Most fish sold as 'Julii Cory' in aquarium stores are actually Corydoras trilineatus (Three-Stripe Cory / False Julii Cory). True C. julii has fine, isolated dark spots on the body and head that do not connect into stripes or a maze-like pattern; C. trilineatus displays three prominent horizontal lines or a connected reticulated pattern. True C. julii is considerably rarer in the trade.

Fine sand substrate is strongly recommended — coarse gravel or sharp substrate can damage the sensitive barbels, which tend to erode and may lead to infection. A minimum substrate depth of around 3 cm allows natural foraging behavior.

Corydoras are facultative air-breathers and have been observed making short dashes to the surface to gulp atmospheric air; this is normal behavior and does not indicate oxygen deficiency, though well-oxygenated water is still preferred.

Copper sensitivity: this species, like other callichthyid catfishes, is sensitive to copper-based medications (e.g., copper sulfate treatments for ich). Standard ich medications with copper should be avoided; alternatives such as heat treatment or ich-X (formalin-based) are generally better tolerated.

C. julii produces a mild toxin from its axillary gland as a defense mechanism; this is not harmful under normal aquarium conditions but handling should be done carefully as the hardened pectoral spines can pierce skin.

Tends to be more active and less shy when kept in groups of 6 or more. Groups smaller than 4 have been reported to show increased stress and hiding behavior. Compatible with other Corydoras species, which may shoal together loosely.

See also: [corydoras-aeneus], [corydoras-sterbai], [corydoras-panda] for sibling species with similar care requirements.
breedingNotes: Egg-layer. Spawning has been reported to be triggered by significant (50–70%) water changes using slightly cooler water, simulating the onset of the rainy season. Males pursue the female, who holds 2–4 eggs between her pelvic fins (the T-position spawning embrace); the female then places adhesive eggs on glass, broad plant leaves, or other surfaces. A ratio of 2–3 males per female is generally recommended. Eggs typically hatch within 3–5 days; newly hatched fry accept microworms and newly hatched brine shrimp nauplii. Adults may eat eggs if left in the tank — a separate breeding tank or egg removal is advisable.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Primary source; Aquarium Co-Op (slice primary) returned 404 for a dedicated C. julii page — SeriouslyFish used as primary per playbook fallback. Covers native range, water parameters, tank size, diet, behavior, and breeding in detail.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 5.2 cm SL), temperature range (23–26°C), pH (6.0–8.0), native range (lower Amazon + NE Brazil coastal rivers), family/order, and habitat. FishBase pH max of 8.0 is slightly higher than SeriouslyFish (7.5) and user brief (7.5); majority of sources agree on 7.5 as practical upper bound so 7.5 retained.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (2–2.5 in = ~5.1–6.4 cm), lifespan (5–7 years), min tank (20 gal = 76 L), pH (6.0–7.5), kH (3–10°), diet, grouping (6+). Temperature upper end noted as 29°C — higher than SeriouslyFish/FishBase consensus; likely accounts for acclimated aquarium specimens. Upper bound averaged to 26°C per SeriouslyFish + FishBase agreement.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op (blog — general Cory care)
additional.notes: Species-specific page not found; general Corydoras care guide used for supplementary diet, substrate, and tank-size guidance. Notes that 'Julii Cory' in trade is frequently C. trilineatus.

fish.breedingNotes: An egg-layer; spawning is generally triggered by large water changes using slightly cooler water. Pairs adopt a T-position embrace, with the female depositing adhesive eggs on glass or broad plant leaves. Eggs typically hatch in three to five days, and fry accept microworms and newly hatched brine shrimp nauplii.
